coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic water splitting to produce hydrogen is one of the most promising strategies to alleviate the energy crisis and environmental pollution. Nevertheless, the rapid recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs in the volume phase and surface of the semiconductor photocatalysis severely limits the activity of the photocatalysts. Herein, the hollow tubular carbon nitride (HTCN) was modified by the introduction of S element doping and anchoring carbon dots (CDs) on the surface to prepare CDs/S-HTCN composite, which exhibits boosted photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate (∼9284 μmol h−1 g−1) compared to and S-HTCN (∼3120 μmol h−1 g−1) and HTCN (∼2086 μmol h−1 g−1). The enhancement of photocatalytic activity is mainly ascribed to the adjusted the electronic structure and optical properties of HTCN by S atoms doping for promoting rapid volume phase charge separation efficiency and captured and stored electrons from conduction band of S-HTCN by CDs loading for accelerating the surface separation efficiency of the electron-hole pairs. This dual-channels separation mechanism of photo-generated charges can provide insights into the development of composite photocatalyst with high activity and stability.

head.ce:title: Dual-channels separated mechanism of photo-generated charges over semiconductor photocatalyst for hydrogen evolution: Interfacial charge transfer and transport dynamics insight

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic water splitting to produce hydrogen is one of the most promising strategies to alleviate the energy crisis and environmental pollution. Nevertheless, the rapid recombination of photo-induced electron-hole pairs in the volume phase and surface of the semiconductor photocatalysis severely limits the activity of the photocatalysts. Herein, the hollow tubular carbon nitride (HTCN) was modified by the introduction of S element doping and anchoring carbon dots (CDs) on the surface to prepare CDs/S-HTCN composite, which exhibits boosted photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate (∼9284 μmol h−1 g−1) compared to and S-HTCN (∼3120 μmol h−1 g−1) and HTCN (∼2086 μmol h−1 g−1). The enhancement of photocatalytic activity is mainly ascribed to the adjusted the electronic structure and optical properties of HTCN by S atoms doping for promoting rapid volume phase charge separation efficiency and captured and stored electrons from conduction band of S-HTCN by CDs loading for accelerating the surface separation efficiency of the electron-hole pairs. This dual-channels separation mechanism of photo-generated charges can provide insights into the development of composite photocatalyst with high activity and stability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) energy is considered as an ideal energy source to solve the energy crisis and environmental problems in the future, and it is an important solution to achieve a “double-carbon policy” in China [1]. Nevertheless, the development and use of hydrogen energy is greatly limited by its disadvantages, such as the inconvenience of hydrogen storage and transportation, the high energy consumption of conventional water electrolysis methods, and the toxic by-products of industrial anthraquinone methods [2]. Photocatalytic technology has been widely valued due to its potential application in solar energy conversion and utilization, which can realize water splitting into H2 in a green, low energy consumption, easy operation and low pollution way [3]. The general mechanism of semiconductor photocatalysis for water splitting was provide in Scheme S1. The semiconductor photocatalyst is excited under light to produce photo-generated electrons and holes, located on the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively. Several transfer paths may occur in semiconductor materials: photo-induced carriers are rapidly recombined (surface recombination and volume recombination processes) with thermal or light energy emission in a very short time (a few nanoseconds) [4]. The photo-generated charges that transferred to the surface of semiconductor can react with surface adsorption substance (water), achieving photocatalytic water splitting. Nevertheless, the disadvantages of traditional semiconductor photocatalysts are the high recombination rate of photo-generated carriers and the slow charges’ migration speed, which seriously affect the photocatalytic performance. Customarily, single-channel charge separation path includes volume separation or surface separation was adopted to effectively inhibit the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs [5]. For dual-channels charge separation pathway from volume and surface, inhibition of the photo-generated charges recombination and acceleration of photoexcited electron-hole pairs transfer can be realized, is more advantageous since dual-channels charge separation in principle improves the charge separation efficiency by 50 %.

Among the massive photocatalysts, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as a metal-free polymer photocatalyst, is recognized as a promising candidate material due to its uncomplicated preparations, suitable water decomposition band gap (∼2.7 eV), excellent chemical properties and high stability [6,7]. Nevertheless, the high recombination rate of the electron-hole pairs over the original g-C3N4 greatly restrict its photocatalytic properties [8]. In order to address this problem, improving crystallinity [9], introducing defects [10] and heteroatomic doping [11] are ordinarily adopted to modulate the electronic structure of g-C3N4 for enhancing the volume separation efficiency of photon-generated carriers. In addition, constructing the g-C3N4-based heterojunctions [12] or anchoring co-catalyst on the surface of g-C3N4 [13] is also an effective method to promote the surface separation efficiency of the electron-hole pairs. However, most current studies only focus on single-channel charge separation modification mechanisms, that is, either volume separation or surface separation, and dual-channels modification mechanisms, namely combining volume separation and surface separation, are rarely reported. In addition, there is relatively little discussion and research on the charge surface dynamics in the photocatalytic field, which helps to improve the overall charge separation and photocatalytic activity [14]. Consequently, it is very meaningful to develop and design g-C3N4-based efficient photocatalyst with dual-channels charge separation mechanisms for surface separation and volume separation in improving photocatalytic H2 production and conduct a detailed exploration of the kinetic processes of charge extraction and utilization [15].

In this work, we first design a novel 0D/1D composites featuring with sulfur-doped hollow tubular g-C3N4 (S-HTCN), decorated with carbon dots (CDs) (named as CDs/S-HTCN) with efficient dual-channels charge separation mechanism of volume phase and surface separation for photocatalytic water splitting into hydrogen. The prepared optimal CDs/S-HTCN material exhibited outstanding hydrogen production rate as much as 9284 μmol h−1 g−1, which is higher than that of HTCN and S-HTCN samples. This unique dual-channels charge separation mechanism combining volume-phase separation and surface separation was investigated in detail based on fine characterization and transient photovoltage (TPV) techniques, and the dynamics of the charge migration were further discussed.
Melamine (C3N3(NH2)3) (A.R. ≥ 99 %) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Trithiocyanuric acid (C3H3N3S3) (95 %) was provided from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co.,ltd..

Briefly, 8 g of urea and 5 g of melamine were dissolved in 80 mL of deionized water and stirred for 3 h. The mixed solution was then transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 20 h. The precursor obtained from the hydrothermal reaction was then washed several times with water and ethanol, and placed in a vacuum oven to dry at 60 °C. The prepared precursors were calcined in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 4 h at a rate of 2.5 °C/min. Afterwards, the pale-yellow powder was obtained hollow tubular g-C3N4 and named as HTCN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sulfur-doped hollow tubular g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 2 g of melamine and 5.62 g of trithiocyanic acid were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water, respectively, then sonicated for 1 h to allow adequate dispersion of above mixture. Two bottles of the above precursors were mixed and stirred on a magnetic stirrer for another 5 h. After mixing evenly, the mixed precursor was transferred to a 100 mL reaction vessel and placed in an oven, and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the precipitate was washed with ethanol and deionized water to remove impurities and dried in a vacuum drying oven. Finally, the dried product was placed into a muffle furnace, calcined at 2.5 °C/min at 550 °C for 4 h, cooled to room temperature and collected. The obtained sulfur-doped hollow tubular g-C3N4 was labelled as S-HTCN.

CDs derived from ginkgo biloba leaves were synthesized by using a typical hydrothermal method based on previous report [16]. In brief, 10 g of fresh ginkgo biloba from Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, and 200 mL of deionized water was added to a 250 mL reaction vessel and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After natural cooling, the liquid product obtained from the reaction was centrifuged to form the brown-yellow CDs solution with the concentration of 22 mg/mL.

The preparation of CDs/S-HTCN composite photocatalyst is based on the same procedure as that of the above-mentioned S-HTCN photocatalyst, the procedure synthetic steps are given in Scheme 1 . For a change, when melamine and trithiocyanic acid are dissolved in deionized water, a certain volume (3, 5 and 7 mL) of the CDs solution is introduced to the above mixed solution and fully mixed. CDs/S-HTCN composite photocatalyst with different CDs ratio were obtained by subsequent hydrothermal and calcination treatment, and samples were named as CDs/S-HTCN-3, CDs/S-HTCN-5 and CDs/S-HTCN-7, respectively.
The specific test operation steps of photocatalytic H2 tests and other corresponding characterizations are provided in the Supporting Information .

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was employed to visualize the microstructure of as-prepared photocatalysts. From Fig.S1, both HTCN and S-HTCN are tubular structures with thin walls of around 20 nm. After doping of sulphur atoms and modification of CDs, CDs/S-HTCN-5 still maintains a tubular structure (Fig. 1 a and b, Fig.S2), while S-HTCN (holes around 30 nm) and CDs/S-HTCN-5 samples have more large holes and slightly thicker walls compared with HTCN, probably due to the production of H2S, C2S, S and other gases from the S-HTCN precursors during high-temperature thermal polymerization process [17,18]. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images at different sizes (Fig. 1c and d, Fig.S2) can more intuitively see that the tubular structures of S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN appear hollow, with the wall thickness of about 30 nm and the uniform distribution of holes around 40 nm on their surfaces. Further observation (Fig. 1e) shows that the CDs are evenly distributed across the pore channels of the S-HTCN. High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) image of Fig. 1f presents a d-spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with of (001) crystal plane of carbon [19,20], which can tentatively confirm the introduction of CDs into the CDs/S-HTCN composite system. Compared with the electron diffraction image of S-HTCN (Fig.S3), the electron diffraction diagram of CDs/S-HTCN-5 (inset of Fig. 1f) has more diffraction rings, which lateral verifies the incorporation of the polycrystalline material (CDs) in the composite [21]. The elemental mapping images of CDs/S-HTCN-5 and S-HTCN are provided in Fig. 1g, respectively. As expected, both samples showed the presence of S-elements, which confirms the successful implementation of S-atom doping, consistent with the results demonstrated by EDS spectrum (Fig.S4). The above characterization results have preliminarily confirmed the initial success of the modification by S-doping and CDs anchoring systems for the modification of HTCN.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) diagram of HTCN possesses obvious diffraction peaks at 13.7° and 27.53° (Fig. 2 a1), corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of graphitic carbonitrides, due to the in-plane structure-filling motifs and characteristic interlayer superimposed reflections of the conjugated aromatic system, respectively [22]. Although the crystal structures of HTCN, S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN are identical, it is noteworthy that the diffraction peaks in the (100) crystal plane (Fig. 2a2) of S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN increasingly less pronounced compared to HTCN, attributed to the periodicity of the planar structure for the fragment since the porous structure [23]. The intensity of the (002) diffraction peak for S-HTCN (Fig. 2a3) is significantly reduced with full width at half maximum, probably due to the hollow nanostructure of S-HTCN, while the (002) peak position of S-HTCN shifted to a larger angle, indicating that the doping of S atoms reduces the crystal plane spacing [24]. With the introduction of different contents of CDs, the peak strength and peak width of CDs/S-HTCN exhibit significantly different patterns from S-HTCN. The (002) crystal plane of CDs/S-HTCN has a higher intensity and narrower peak width, confirming a relatively strong interaction between the large aromatic supramolecular layers of π-π stacking, while the shift of the (002) peak position of CDs/S-HTCN to a larger angle may be due to the introduction of CDs for enabling a higher crystallinity [25], smaller layer spacing and greatly facilitate interlayer charge transfer [26]. The molecular structure of the prepared samples was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 2b1, all samples have almost identical characteristic peaks, indicating that neither the doping of S atoms nor the introduction of CDs has altered the molecular structure of HTCN. The peak at 812 cm−1 is the unique breathing vibration of heptazine ring in g-C3N4 (Fig. 2b2), and the peaks at 1200–1650 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration mode of the CN/CN heterocycles (Fig. 2b3), while the peaks around 2900 ∼ 3300 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of N-Hx (x = 1 or 2) [27–29]. In addition, no new other peaks were produced in the FT-IR spectra, which may result from the relatively limited CDs amount in the CDs/S-HTCN composite system [30].

Further chemical information and chemical valences of as-prepared samples were obtained from the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As exhibited in the survey spectra of Fig. S5a and Table S1, all the samples of HTCN, S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN-5 consisted of three basal elements, including C, N, and O. In addition, the presence of low amount S elements in S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN-5 can be found due to the introduction of trithiocyanuric acid in the process of synthesis. Fig. 3 a shows the C 1s spectra of the S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN-5, and the peak can be deconvolved into five peaks (see Table S2), corresponding to CSC, CC/CC, CO, N-CN and C-NHx (x = 1 or 2) [31,32]. Compared with C 1s of pure HTCN (Fig. S5b), the new peaks ascribed to CSC can be found in S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN-5, confirming the successful doping of S elements in the S-HTCN samples [33]. As displayed in Fig. 3b and Fig. S5c, it can be found that the N 1s spectra are divided into four characteristic peaks of CNC, N-(C)3, CNH and π excitation (see Table S3) [34,35]. Fig. 3c and Fig. S5d exhibit three main peaks obtained from high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra (see Table S4), corresponding to OH, CO and CO, respectively [36], while the peaks strength of OH and CO over CDs/S-HTCN-5 is significantly enhanced compared to S-HTCN, which may be attributed to the role of Oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs [37]. The S 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows a faint elemental S signal, which is related to the doping of S elements in this system, but the amount of S atoms doped is so small that the spectrum is not significant [38]. Meanwhile, the C 1s, O 1s and S 2p XPS peaks of CDs/S-HTCN-5 moved to lower or higher binding energy positions compared with S-HTCN, confirming the change in the electron cloud density due to the introduction of CDs.

Predictably, the optical properties of pristine HTCN could be changed greatly after the doping of sulfur atoms and the introduction of CDs. Fig. 4 a exhibits the UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra of HTCN, S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN composites. It can be seen that the compared with HTCN, the obvious absorption tails in the adsorption range of 400–700 nm can be found in S-HTCN, and the absorption edge is also significantly red-shifted due to the formation of cavity structure, the large holes and rough pipe wall formed by S doping, demonstrating that S atomic doping plays a major role in the optical properties of HTCN and in changing the band gap of HTCN [39]. Furthermore, notably, after the introduction of CDs in CDs/S-HTCN composites, the color of powder samples gradually became darker from the original brown to gray-black (Fig.S6). Thus, the light absorption capacity of the CDs/S-HTCN composites is further enhanced due to the strong light scattering and capturing effects of the CDs [40]. Fig. 4b provides the band gap values of the as-prepared samples determined based on the UV–visible diffuse spectral conversion of the Kubelka-Munk function [41]. The calculated band gaps of HTCN, S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN-5 are 2.76, 2.61 and 2.56 eV, respectively, which are consistent with results of Fig. 4a, confirming that the S element doping is indeed the main reason for changing the band gap [42]. To obtain the conduction band (CB) positions of as-prepared samples, Mott-Schottky curves (Fig. 4c) with three different frequencies of 800, 1000 and 1200 Hz were analyzed (versus Ag/AgCl reference electrodes), resulting in −0.54, −0.61 and −0.69 eV for HTCN, S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN-5, respectively. The CB of the CDs/S-HTCN-5 has a negative shift of 0.15 eV compared to the original HTCN, resulting in an enhanced thermodynamic driving force for the photocatalytic reduction of protons into H2 [43]. According to as-obtained CB positions and band gap values, the band structure diagrams of the samples can be drawn in Fig. 4d. Obviously, the positions of both CB and valence band (VB) of HTCN can be changed by S doping and CDs, and all catalysts meet the thermodynamic conditions of the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction level (2H + 2e-=H2, E0 = 0.0 V vs RHE) [44,45]. In this regard, the CDs/S-HTCN composite is an ideal photocatalyst with a broad spectral response and strong activation energy level of photocatalytic hydrogen production under solar light irradiation.

The photocatalytic performances of hydrogen evolution over as-prepared HTCN, S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN photocatalysts were determined in an aqueous solution with containing 10 vol% triethanolamine (TEOA) as the sacrificial agent and 1 wt% Pt as the co-catalyst, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 a, the hydrogen production rate of HTCN reaches 4172 μmol/g using 25 mg of catalyst within 2 h irradiation. Compared with HTCN, the photocatalytic H2 production of S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN composites have been obviously enhanced due to the doping of sulfur elements and introduction of CDs, which helps to enhance the elevating CB potential and promote the utilization of light of HTCN [46]. In terms of the hydrogen production rates (Fig. 5b), the optimal CDs/S-HTCN-5 sample is 9284 μmol h−1 g−1, which is 2.97 and 4.45 times higher than S-HTCN and HTCN photocatalysts, respectively. To judge the properties of our synthetic materials more clearly, apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) values at different wavelength were recorded [47]. As given in Fig. 5c, the AQE value at 420 nm over CDs/S-HTCN-5 sample is up to 1.47 %, which is superior to other CN-based samples reported in recent years (Table S5). In practice, the cycle stability of the sample is a very meaningful evaluation index for the catalyst. As expected, the cycle reaction test results in Fig. 5d exhibited that the hydrogen production performance of as-prepared CDs/S-HTCN-5 has not been decreased significantly. The XRD patterns (Fig. 5e) and SEM images (Fig. 5f) were essentially the same before and after the reaction, indicating that the microstructure of the reaction was not destroyed, and the above conclusion confirmed the excellent stability of the CD/S-HTCN composite photocatalyst.

Photoluminescence (PL) is used to measure the efficiency of photo-induced carriers’ separation, transfer, and migration. Fig. 6 a shows the peak intensities of PL spectra over CDs/S-HTCN composites are significantly lower than that of S-HTCN and HTCN, suggesting that the introduction of the sulfur element and CDs promotes a further enhancement of the charge separation of HTCN [48]. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy also confirmed the improved charge separation efficiency of as-prepared HTCN, S-HTCN, and CDs/S-HTCN-5. TRPL decay curves in Fig. 6b can be fitted based on the three-exponential function in the following equation (τ) (Eq. (1)) [49]:

The fitting results shows that the CDs/S-HTCN-5 possesses a significantly increased singlet exciton lifetime (8.01 ns), which is higher than that of S-HTCN (7.58 ns) and HTCN (7.32 ns), indicating an accelerated exciton dissociation efficiency, the low electron hole composite rate leads to the increase of the lone pair electron concentration [50]. The effective charge separation of CDs/S-HTCN-5 composites was also verified by transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As exhibited in Fig. 6c, the photocurrent curve of CDs/S-HTCN-5 is significantly higher than that of S-HTCN and much higher than that of HTCN, demonstrating that combination of doping of S atoms and decoration of CDs can not only rise the reduction potential of HTCN, but also greatly improve the photocurrent response and produce more charge carriers for participating in photocatalytic H2 evolution reaction [51]. As for electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) in Fig. 6d, the impedance spectrum radius of S-HTCN is significantly larger than that of CDs/S-HTCN-5 and smaller than that of HTCN, indicating that the doping of S element can effectively enhance the charge transfer ability of HTCN, and the introduction of CDs expands the advantage of low impedance and contributes to the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers [52]. The overpotentials for H2 evolution of the as-prepared samples were tested by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and the results are shown in Fig. 6e. The overpotential required for the HTCN to reach 10 mA cm−2 is −2.12 V, while the overpotentials of S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN-5 were significantly reduced to −2.0 and −1.95 V, demonstrating that S-doping and CDS modification can effectively improve the kinetics of photocatalytic H2 production [53,54]. Subsequently, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas and pore-size distributions of as-prepared samples were obtained by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm. As displayed in Fig. 6f, all samples exhibited type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, indicating the presence of mesoporous structure [55,56], and corresponding average size distributions were given in Fig.S7. In addition, BET specific surface areas of HTCN, S-HTCN and CDs/S-HTCN-5 are 33.84, 79.74 and 114.09 m2/g, respectively. The increase in the specific surface area of S-HTCN over HTCN can definitely explain the doping of S atoms produces H2S, C2S and S gases during thermal polymerization process [57], promoting the porous structure of HTCN. It also reveals that the CDs surface itself provides more surface-active sites on S-HTCN for photocatalysis of water molecules reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamics of the charge migration of as-prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the connection between the performance of the prepared photocatalysts and the interfacial charge transfer dynamics, transient photovoltage (TPV) test was performed. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the photovoltage intensity of CDs/S-HTCN-5 and S-HTCN is significantly higher than that of HTCN, confirming that both S element doping and CDs enable HTCN to obtain higher electron-hole separation efficiency, and CDs have the characteristics as electron acceptors [58], which is the main reason for the great improvement of photo-induced charges’ separation efficiency in this composite system. The local amplification and area (A) integration of the original TPV curve could have analyzed the maximum charge extraction time and the maximum charge extraction amount of as-prepared samples (Fig. 7b and c). As given in Fig. 7b, the charge extraction time of S-HTCN (tmax2 ) is slightly shorter relative to HTCN (tmax1 ), while the charge extraction time of CDs/S-HTCN-5 (tmax3 ) is significantly shortened, confirming that the introduction of CDs greatly promotes the electron extraction and improves the electron transfer efficiency at the interface [59]. Corresponding to Fig. 7c, S-HTCN (A2 = 0.061) is 2 times higher than HTCN (A1 = 0.03), indicating that S atom doping promotes charge volume separation in the network of CN, and the volume separation of charge is the rapid step of surface separation [60]. After the introduction of CDs, the charge extraction amount of CDs/S-HTCN-5 (A3 = 0.09) is 1.5 times and 3 times higher compared with S-HTCN and HTCN, which confirms that most electrons quickly transfer to the surface of CDs, reflecting the characteristics of CDs as electron acceptors, and promoting the surface separation efficiency of charge [61]. As exhibited in Fig. 7d, the electron decay constants of CDs/S-HTCN-5 (τ3 = 1.000 ms) were higher than S-HTCN (τ2 = 0.752 ms) and HTCN (τ1 = 0.696 ms), indicating that more photo-generated charge is retained on the surface of the CDs/S-HTCN-5 sample [62]. In addition, the surface effective electrons of the catalyst (ne = A*τ/tmax) [63] were calculated and are listed in Table 1 . The ne of CDs/S-HTCN-5 (ne = 0.083) and S-HTCN (ne = 0.041) increased than the surface electrons of the original HTCN (ne = 0.019). The above conclusions indicate that the charge dual separation mechanism plays an important role in improving the hydrogen production performance for the CN-based semiconductor photocatalyst.

H 2
and ne are still satisfy the linear relationship, suggesting that the semi-reaction of holes’ sacrifice is not the rate-limiting step in this work. Photocatalytic efficiency depends on the intensity of light, the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs, electron interface transfer (interface reaction rate), reactant concentration, etc. As is well known, the general process of HER can be divided into two pathways: Volmer-Heyrovsky or Volmer-Tafel processes [64]. The initial steps of both two processes are the same, namely the catalyst adsorbed proton in water to produce H atoms (H++ catalyst → H*). While the difference is in the later steps, one is the as-produced hydrogen atom is caused to combine with water residual proton to generate hydrogen (H*+H+→H2), namely the Heyrovsky process; the other is the combination between two hydrogen atoms to produce hydrogen (H*+H*→H2), which called Tafel process [65]. In light of TPV results and above apparent electric charge kinetic analysis, the hydrogen production rate shows a linear relationship with ne or Ce , which can indicate that the hydrogen production center of this system follows the Volmer-Heyrovsky process [65,66]. The Tafel slope of as-prepared samples can be obtained based on the electrochemical HER analysis and shown in Fig. 8b. In general, the Tafel slope values between 40 and 70 mV/dec satisfy the characteristics of Volmer-Heyrovsky process. Notably, the Tafel slope value of CDs/S-HTCN-5 (40.1 mV/dec) is lower than HTCN (63.3 mV/dec) and S-HTCN (45.7 mV/dec), indicating that the introduction of CDs significantly improves the apparent dynamics of this composite system and may be the main reason for its enhanced reactivity promotion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channels charge separation mechanism of CDs/S-HTCN
ce:section.ce:para: According to the various basic characterization and tests mentioned above, the potential working mechanism of CDs/S-HTCN with excellent photocatalytic hydrogen production performance was proposed and given in Scheme 2 . The significantly improved performance of CDs/S-HTCN is attributed to dual-channels separated mechanism of the photo-induced charge carriers in the photocatalytic system. For one reason, from the perspective of volume phase charge separation, the doping of S atoms can greatly adjust the electronic structure and optical properties of HTCN, which makes it obtain a more negative guide position and improves the redox capacity, thus bringing a fast volume phase separation efficiency [67]. In addition, with the doping of sulfur atoms, a large amount of gas in the reaction process makes the hollow tube structure rough and porous, multiple reflections of the incident light, which not only greatly improves the utilization efficiency of light, but also prompts the longitudinal migration of the photo-generated charges in the flexible walls, thus greatly extending the life of them. Another reason for the significant improvement in the photocatalytic activity of this system is the introduction of CDs, which greatly promotes the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs on the surface of catalyst. Moreover, TPV tests and kinetic analysis of the charge separation revealed that the essential reason for the significant improvement in photocatalytic HER performance over CDs/S-HTCN composite system is the increase of the effective electron concentration on the surface of photocatalyst. Sunlight exposure on the S-HTCN surface produces electron-hole pairs, and the electrons in the CB are easily captured and stored by the CDs, accelerating the charge separation efficiency, and improving the photocatalytic activity of CDs/S-HTCN. In addition, the surface of CDs also contains rich active sites, in which electrons are bound to H+ to generate H2 via a Volmer-Heyrovsky process. The holes left on VB are oxidized by TEOA, which effectively suppresses the photo-induced electron-hole pair recombination and improves the efficiency of electron-utilization.

In conclusion, we demonstrate a dual-channels separation mechanism of photo-generated charges from volume and surface of HTCN by introduction of S-atoms doping and CDs for enhancing the sunlight-driven photocatalytic hydrogen activity. Detailed characterizations reveal that the doping of S-atoms not only can greatly adjust the electronic structure and optical properties of HTCN for achieving rapid charge separation efficiency from volume phase and improving the redox capacity, but also endow the porous hollow tubular structure for increasing the specific surface area and exposing more active sites of HTCN. Additionally, CDs could effectively capture and store electrons from the CB of S-HTCN, greatly promoting the separation efficiency of the electron hole on the surface of photocatalyst to achieve an impressive photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of up to 9284 μmol h−1 g−1. This study presents a strategy of dual-channels charges separation to modify semiconductor for achieving highly active photocatalysts in the utilization and conversion of solar energy. Meanwhile, this work provides a quasi-quantitative apparent kinetics discussion based on analysis of details over charge extraction and utilization in photocatalytic hydrogen production with the electrocatalytic HER process, which present an important reference for photocatalyst design and activity promotion in the future.
